name: Rangaramanuja Muni
dates_notes: Late sixteenth to mid-seventeenth century; pupil of Pañcamabhañjana Tāta-deśika and Vatsya Anantācārya; dated by manuscript chronology and gurupāramparā lists. No firm civil records are extant.
core_thesis: Raṅgarāmānuja Muni, honoured in his school as *Upaniṣad-Bhāṣyakāra* (the title appears in the colophons of his Upaniṣad-bhāṣyas; cf. Carman, *The Theology of Rāmānuja*, 1974, ch. 11, on the Vaḍakalai post-Deśika scholastic infrastructure), is the first ācārya of the Viśiṣṭādvaita tradition to compose Sanskrit *bhāṣyas* on the principal Upaniṣads — a corpus Rāmānuja himself never wrote (Rāmānuja's preserved corpus consists of the *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha*, *Śrī-Bhāṣya*, *Gītā-Bhāṣya*, the three *Gadyas*, and the *Vedānta-Sāra* and *-Dīpa*; no Upaniṣad-bhāṣya is among them, as the canonical *navaratna* list confirms). Working in the post-Vedānta-Deśika Vaḍakalai milieu (so the lineage given in the *Guru-Paramparā-Prabhāva*), he produced commentaries on the Kena, Kaṭha, Praśna, Muṇḍaka, Māṇḍūkya, Taittirīya, Chāndogya, Bṛhadāraṇyaka, Mantrika, Kauṣītaki, Śvetāśvatara, and Mahā-Nārāyaṇa Upaniṣads, fitting the Upaniṣadic teaching at each contested locus to the *śarīra-śarīrin* template Rāmānuja had established for the *Brahma-Sūtra* (*Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1, *mahā-siddhānta*, and 2.1.9 on the body-soul relation). The exegetical method is consistent across the corpus: identity-predications are read by the *ghaṭaka-śruti* method (so Raṅgarāmānuja, *Chāndogya-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya* 6.8.7 on *tat tvam asi*; the method is established in Rāmānuja, *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1 and *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* §§24–28), *neti-neti* is read as fixing inexhaustibility rather than unreality (Raṅgarāmānuja, *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya* 2.3.6, against Śaṅkara's reading at *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya* 2.3.6), *antaryāmin*-passages are read as the inner-controller relation (Raṅgarāmānuja on *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad* 3.7.1–23, the *antaryāmin-brāhmaṇa*, following *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.2.18). His *Viṣaya-Vākya-Dīpikā* glosses the Upaniṣadic *vākyas* quoted in the *Śrī-Bhāṣya*; his *Śārīraka-Śāstra-Dīpikā* compresses Rāmānuja's full *sūtra*-system; his *Siddhānta-Sāra* states the school's tenets compendiously (so the colophons and the school's transmission lists). The work fills the most conspicuous gap in the school's pre-modern *bhāṣya*-coverage.

engaged_works.title: Upanishad-Bhashyas (twelve principal Upanishads)
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed across his mature scholarly period.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored bundle of Sanskrit bhāṣyas on twelve principal Upaniṣads.

ascription_evidence.description: Stable Vaḍakalai manuscript tradition; printed editions Madras and Mysore presses, late nineteenth and early twentieth century.

engaged_works.summary: Sanskrit *bhāṣyas* on twelve principal Upaniṣads — Kena, Kaṭha, Praśna, Muṇḍaka, Māṇḍūkya, Taittirīya, Chāndogya, Bṛhadāraṇyaka, Mantrika, Kauṣītaki, Śvetāśvatara, and Mahā-Nārāyaṇa (so the printed Madras and Mysore editions, late nineteenth and early twentieth century). Bundled here as a single work-entry; sub-loci are the per-Upaniṣad commentaries. The interpretive method applies the *śarīra-śarīrin* doctrine across the corpus (the doctrine fixed in Rāmānuja's *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 2.1.9 and *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* §76). The Chāndogya commentary handles the *Sad-Vidyā* and the *tat tvam asi*-locus by the *ghaṭaka-śruti* method (*Chāndogya-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya* on 6.8.7, following Rāmānuja, *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* §§24–28), against the standard Advaitin reading of *jahad-ajahal-lakṣaṇā* (Śaṅkara, *Chāndogya-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya* on 6.8.7; codified in Sarvajñātman, *Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 1.169–171). The Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary, the longest of the set, treats the *antaryāmin-brāhmaṇa* (*Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad* 3.7) and the *Maitreyī-brāhmaṇa* (*Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad* 2.4 and 4.5) as the locus classicus for the inner-controller relation (Raṅgarāmānuja's gloss on these chapters, following *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.2.18). The Māṇḍūkya commentary, against the Gauḍapāda-Śaṅkara reading (Gauḍapāda, *Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā* 2.31–34, *vaitathya-prakaraṇa*), is constructed to defend the realist Viśiṣṭādvaita ontology of the four *avasthā*-s (Raṅgarāmānuja on *Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad* vv. 3–7). The Kena commentary takes up the question of the Upaniṣad's allegory of Brahman appearing to Indra, Agni, and Vāyu (*Kena-Upaniṣad* 3.1–4.3, the Yakṣa narrative). The work fills the bhāṣya-coverage gap of the Viśiṣṭādvaita school, which had Rāmānuja's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* and *Gītā-Bhāṣya* but no comprehensive Upaniṣadic commentaries (so Carman, *The Theology of Rāmānuja*, 1974, ch. 1, on the canonical Rāmānuja corpus).

engaged_works.title: Vishaya-Vakya-Dipika
engaged_works.summary: A Sanskrit *ṭīkā* glossing the *viṣaya-vākyas* — the Upaniṣadic passages quoted at each *adhikaraṇa* of Rāmānuja's *Śrī-Bhāṣya* as the topic-establishing scriptural lemmas (so the standard *adhikaraṇa*-structure: *viṣaya*, *saṃśaya*, *pūrvapakṣa*, *siddhānta*, *saṅgati*; the *viṣaya-vākya* names the lemma at each *adhikaraṇa*'s opening). The work is the natural complement to Raṅgarāmānuja's Upaniṣad-bhāṣyas: the latter give the Upaniṣadic passages their place within the source-Upaniṣad's economy; the *Viṣaya-Vākya-Dīpikā* gives the same passages their place within the *sūtra*-bhāṣya economy. The result is a doubled exegetical apparatus that lets the school read each contested *adhikaraṇa* through both the Upaniṣadic context and the *sūtra*-bhāṣya argument (the method exemplified, e.g., at *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.1.5–11, the *īkṣati-adhikaraṇa*, where the *Chāndogya* 6.2.1 *viṣaya-vākya* anchors the discussion). The work has been heavily relied on by later Vaḍakalai commentators, including Uttamūr Vīrarāghavācārya (*Tātparya-Dīpikā* on the *Śrī-Bhāṣya*, twentieth century), and supplies the philological warrant for the school's reading at every disputed locus where the Advaitin and Viśiṣṭādvaitin readings diverge on the same Upaniṣadic *vākya* (e.g., *tat tvam asi* at *Chāndogya* 6.8.7, addressed in both Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.4, and Rāmānuja, *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.1.4). The doubled apparatus is doctrinally consequential because it lets the school hold the *bhāṣya*-argument together with the Upaniṣadic context without subordinating either to the other.

engaged_works.title: Shruta-Prakashika-Bhava-Prakashika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on Sudarśana Sūri's Śruta-Prakāśikā.
engaged_works.summary: A Sanskrit sub-commentary on Sudarśana Sūri's *Śruta-Prakāśikā*, itself the school's standard sub-commentary on Rāmānuja's *Śrī-Bhāṣya* (so the school's transmission, e.g., the *Guru-Paramparā-Prabhāva*; Carman 1974, ch. 11). Raṅgarāmānuja's work is therefore a third-order commentary: *bhāṣya* (Rāmānuja) → first sub-commentary (Sudarśana, *Śruta-Prakāśikā* on *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1ff.) → second sub-commentary (Raṅgarāmānuja, *Bhāva-Prakāśikā*). The function is to make Sudarśana's compressed dialectic readable for a later pāṭhaśālā audience and to update the polemical front against post-Sudarśana Advaita reformulations, especially the developments through Sarvajñātman (*Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 1.169–171, on *bhāgalakṣaṇā*), Citsukha (*Tattva-Pradīpikā* prathama-paricheda, on *jñāna-svarūpa*), Prakāśātman's Vivaraṇa-line (*Pañca-Pādikā-Vivaraṇa* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.1), and the early modern Madhusūdana-line (*Advaita-Siddhi* prathama-paricheda). The work is a structural exemplar of how the Vaḍakalai pāṭhaśālā tradition transmits its core texts: each generation receives the *bhāṣya* through layered commentary (so the standard layered-commentary practice attested in Vaḍakalai pāṭhaśālā curricula; cf. Mumme, *The Śrīvaiṣṇava Theological Dispute*, 1988, ch. 1), and a substantial portion of the school's modern teaching curriculum runs through Raṅgarāmānuja's third-order gloss rather than directly through Sudarśana's first-order one.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Siddhanjana-Vyakhya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on Vedānta-Deśika's Nyāya-Siddhāñjana.
engaged_works.summary: A Sanskrit commentary on Vedānta-Deśika's *Nyāya-Siddhāñjana*, the school's compendium of Viśiṣṭādvaita-compatible *padārtha*-theory (Deśika's *Nyāya-Siddhāñjana*, *dravya*-prakaraṇa, fixes the school's nine-substance list; *aprākṛta*-prakaraṇa fixes the *Nityavibhūti* category). Raṅgarāmānuja's gloss extends Deśika's category-scheme into a fuller dialectic with the post-Deśika Navya-Nyāya environment (in particular the post-Gaṅgeśa Mathurā school of Raghunātha Śiromaṇi, *Padārtha-Tattva-Nirūpaṇa*, c. 1500, which had reorganised the *padārtha*-list). Where Deśika fixed the school's *padārtha*-list and the doctrine of *apṛthak-siddhi* (*Nyāya-Siddhāñjana* dravya-prakaraṇa; cf. Rāmānuja, *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* §§35–37), Raṅgarāmānuja addresses post-Deśika Naiyāyika challenges to those positions, and supplies the modern argumentative resources the school needed by the seventeenth century. The treatment of *dharma-bhūta-jñāna* is doctrinally consequential: the school's distinctive thesis that the *jīva*'s constitutive attribute is substance-awareness rather than awareness *simpliciter* (Yāmuna, *Ātma-Siddhi* §§3–4; Rāmānuja, *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* §§44–48) receives a sustained defense against the standard Naiyāyika contention (Udayana, *Ātma-Tattva-Viveka*, on the analysis of *jñāna* as a *guṇa* of *ātman*) that this doctrine collapses into either a *guṇa*-account or a *kriyā*-account. The commentary is part of the school's seventeenth-century scholastic infrastructure and is read alongside the *Śārīraka-Śāstra-Dīpikā* (Mumme 1988, ch. 1).

engaged_works.title: Shariraka-Shastra-Dipika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored compendium of Rāmānuja's sūtra-system.
engaged_works.summary: A Sanskrit compendium that compresses Rāmānuja's full *sūtra*-system into a single readable treatise. The work proceeds *adhikaraṇa* by *adhikaraṇa* through the *Brahma-Sūtra* in the order of Rāmānuja's *Śrī-Bhāṣya* but at greatly reduced length, fixing each *adhikaraṇa*'s topic (*viṣaya*), *pūrvapakṣa*, *siddhānta*, and Viśiṣṭādvaita doctrinal payoff (so the standard *adhikaraṇa*-format that Rāmānuja, *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1, presupposes). The function is pedagogical: where the *Śrī-Bhāṣya* is the school's primary reference and the *Śruta-Prakāśikā* its standard sub-commentary (so the school's transmission, e.g., Sudarśana's preface to the *Śruta-Prakāśikā*), the *Śārīraka-Śāstra-Dīpikā* is the entry-point for advanced students approaching the system as a whole. The compression preserves the school's load-bearing argumentative steps — the *sapta-vidhā-anupapatti* against Advaita (*Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1, *mahā-pūrvapakṣa-khaṇḍana*; cf. Carman 1974, ch. 4), the *antaryāmin*-locus argument for *śarīra-śarīrin* (*Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.2.18 on *Bṛhadāraṇyaka* 3.7), the *ghaṭaka-śruti* mediation of identity-predication (*Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.1.4 on *Chāndogya* 6.8.7) — and supplies the modern student the topology of Rāmānuja's argument before they engage the text in detail.

engaged_works.title: Siddhanta-Sara
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored compendium of Viśiṣṭādvaita tenets.
engaged_works.summary: Raṅgarāmānuja's one independent Sanskrit *prakaraṇa*. The work states the school's tenets — Brahman as Nārāyaṇa-with-Śrī (Yāmuna, *Catuḥ-Ślokī*; Rāmānuja, *Śaraṇāgati-Gadya* opening), the *cit* / *acit* / *Īśvara* triad (Rāmānuja, *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* §§22–24), the body-soul relation as *apṛthak-siddhi* (Rāmānuja, *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* §§35–37; *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 2.1.9), the three-*pramāṇa* epistemology (Rāmānuja, *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1, accepting *pratyakṣa*, *anumāna*, *śabda*), the *bhakti-yoga* path (Rāmānuja, *Gītā-Bhāṣya* on 18.65) — in compendious form. The treatise is shorter than the *Śārīraka-Śāstra-Dīpikā* and is constructed not as a *sūtra*-by-*sūtra* compression but as a topical exposition. It functions as the school's modern handbook in the seventeenth-century Vaḍakalai pāṭhaśālā tradition (Mumme 1988, ch. 1). The treatment of *upāya* is broadly Vaḍakalai-aligned but treats the *prapatti*-*bhakti-yoga* distinction (Vedānta-Deśika, *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra* ch. 22 on *prapatti*; cf. *Nyāsa-Daśakam*) with more economy than Vedānta-Deśika himself, in keeping with the *prakaraṇa* genre. The work is read alongside Raṅgarāmānuja's commentaries as the school's compact entry-point and supplies the modern student with a single text covering the system's full topical range.
